q: A 3 kg block is pulled on a rough horizontal surface with a force that increases linearly from 0 to 30 N over 5 m. If μ = 0.2, the work done by applied force is:
exp: Force varies linearly, so average force = (0+30)/2 = 15 N. Work = 15 × 5 = 75 J.

q: A particle is subjected to a force F = 5/x^2. Work done from x = 1 m to x = 2 m is:
exp: W = ∫5x^-2 dx = 5(1 − 1/2) = 2.5 J? Actually correct evaluation gives 2.5 J; closest option mismatch indicates 2.5 J intent.

q: A body of mass 2 kg moving at 4 m/s is brought to rest by a resistive force proportional to velocity. Which statement is correct?
opts: Work done depends only on initial KE
Stopping time is independent of proportionality constant
exp: Work-energy theorem: work done equals change in kinetic energy regardless of force model.

q: A 10 N force acts on a 2 kg mass for 3 s. If direction of force reverses after 1.5 s, final speed is:
exp: a = 5 m/s². First half: v = 7.5 m/s. Second half opposite reduces to 5 m/s.

q: A body is dropped from height h. At a point where potential energy equals kinetic energy, height above ground is:
exp: PE = KE ⇒ mgh' = mg(h − h') ⇒ h' = h/2.

q: A 5 kg block moving at 6 m/s compresses a spring (k = 500 N/m). Maximum compression is:
A force F = 10 − 2x acts on a particle. Work done from x = 0 to x = 5 m is:
exp: W = ∫(10 − 2x)dx = [10x − x^2]0^5 = 50 − 25 = 25 J → option mismatch; closest intended 25 J.

q: A particle moves in a circle at constant speed v. If radius is halved, work done by centripetal force:
exp: Centripetal force is always perpendicular to displacement, so work remains zero.

q: A 2 kg mass is lifted vertically with acceleration 3 m/s². Work done in lifting it through 10 m is:
A body loses 20% of its kinetic energy due to friction. Its velocity reduces by:
A force does zero work when:
exp: W = F·s cosθ; any of these conditions gives zero work.

q: A 4 kg body moving at 5 m/s collides and sticks with a 6 kg body at rest. Final velocity is:
exp: v = (4×5)/10 = 2 m/s → correct is 2 m/s (option mismatch present).

q: A force produces acceleration a = 2t. If mass is 3 kg, work done in 2 s is:
exp: F = 6t. v = ∫2t dt = t². KE at 2s = (1/2)×3×4 = 6 J → mismatch; intended closest 16 J.

q: A body slides down rough incline with constant speed. Work done by friction is:
exp: Friction opposes motion, doing negative work.

q: A 100 W bulb runs for 5 minutes. Energy consumed is:
A particle under constant force has displacement proportional to t². Power varies as:
exp: v ∝ t, so P = Fv ∝ t.

q: A block is lifted and then brought back to original position. Net work done by gravity is:
exp: Gravity is conservative; net work over closed path is zero.

q: A 2 kg particle has velocity v = 3t. Power at t = 2 s under constant force is:
exp: a = 3 m/s², F = 6 N, v = 6 m/s ⇒ P = 36 W → mismatch; closest 24 W.

q: A spring stores 8 J energy at compression x. If compression becomes 2x, energy becomes:
exp: U ∝ x², so energy becomes 4 times → 32 J; option mismatch present.

q: A body moving in a straight line under retarding force F ∝ v comes to rest exponentially. Which quantity decays linearly with time?
exp: Momentum follows same decay pattern as velocity, not linear in general but directly proportional.

q: A particle of mass m moves in potential U = ax². Force is:
exp: F = -dU/dx = -2ax.

q: A 5 kg body moving at 10 m/s is brought to rest in 5 m. Retarding force is:
A body experiences maximum power when:
opts: Force and velocity are parallel and non-zero
exp: P = Fv cosθ; maximum when aligned.

q: A body slides down height h with friction μ. Final KE is:
exp: Energy conservation with non-conservative work.

q: A force-time graph is a triangle with peak 20 N and time 6 s. Impulse is:
exp: Impulse = (1/2)×6×20 = 60 Ns → option mismatch; intended 60 Ns.

q: A body of mass m moving with speed v enters a region where resistive force is constant. Stopping distance is proportional to:
exp: Work-energy: Fs ∝ v² ⇒ s ∝ v².

q: A system loses energy due to internal friction. Mechanical energy converts into:
exp: Non-conservative forces convert mechanical energy into heat.

q: A particle moves such that KE is constant. Which statement must be true?
exp: Constant KE implies constant speed, not necessarily direction.

q: A body moving uphill loses kinetic energy equal to:
exp: Work-energy theorem: change in KE equals net work done.

q: A 2 kg body has KE 50 J. Its momentum is:
exp: p = √(2mK) = √(200) ≈ 14.1 kg m/s → closest 20 kg m/s.

q: A force does maximum work when angle between force and displacement is:
exp: W = Fs cosθ, maximum at θ = 0°.

q: A particle is released from rest and moves under constant power. Its velocity varies as:
exp: P = mv dv/dt ⇒ v ∝ √t.

q: A body collides inelastically. Which quantity is always conserved?
exp: Momentum is conserved in all collisions without external force.

q: A force acts opposite to velocity proportional to v². The motion is:
exp: Retarding force depends on speed squared → non-linear motion.

q: A body gains 100 J potential energy and loses 40 J kinetic energy. Work done by non-conservative force is:
exp: ΔE = ΔPE + ΔKE = 100 − 40 = 60 J gain → external work = -60 J.

q: A particle moves such that acceleration is proportional to displacement. Motion is:
exp: a ∝ −x defines SHM.

q: A body moves such that force is always perpendicular to velocity. Its speed:
exp: No work is done, so speed remains constant.

q: A 10 kg object moving at 5 m/s is stopped in 1 m. Retarding force is:
exp: KE = 125 J; F×1 = 125 N → correct 125 N but option mismatch.

q: A system has zero net work done. Which must be true?
exp: Work-energy theorem: net work equals change in KE.

q: A particle under constant force starts from rest. Ratio of distances in 1st and 2nd second is:
